title: Pipes and Cisterns Quiz
objectives: Convert pipe times to hourly rates
Compute net rates for combined inlet/outlet systems

questions.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 10 hours. What is its rate per hour?
questions.explanation: Rate = 1 tank / 10 hours = 1/10 tank per hour.

questions.question: Pipe B empties a tank in 20 hours. What is its hourly rate?
questions.explanation: Emptying removes water, so the rate is negative: -1/20 tank per hour.

questions.question: A pipe fills 1/4 of a tank in 2 hours. How many hours to fill the whole tank?
questions.explanation: If 1/4 tank is filled in 2 hours, full tank takes 2 x 4 = 8 hours.

questions.question: If a pipe fills 3/5 of a tank in 6 hours, what is the time for the whole tank?
questions.explanation: Rate = 3/5 / 6 = 1/10 tank per hour, so full tank = 10 hours.

questions.question: Two inlet pipes fill a tank in 8 hours when opened together. One pipe alone takes 12 hours. How long does the other pipe take?
questions.explanation: Combined rate = 1/8. One pipe rate = 1/12. Other = 1/8 - 1/12 = 1/24, so 24 hours.

questions.question: A pipe fills a tank in 15 hours and a second pipe fills it in 10 hours. How long together?
questions.explanation: Rates sum to 1/15 + 1/10 = 1/6, so time = 6 hours.

questions.question: If a pipe works for 4 hours and fills 1/5 of the tank, what is its rate?
Which expression gives the net rate of two inlet pipes A and B?
questions.explanation: When both inlets work together, their rates add.

questions.question: A tank has one inlet pipe of rate 1/12 and one outlet pipe of rate 1/20. What is the net rate?
A leak removes water at 1/15 tank per hour. What is its sign in net rate?
questions.explanation: Leak reduces tank volume, so its rate is negative.

questions.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 20 hours, pipe B in 30 hours. Both working together fill the tank in:
A pipe fills 1/3 tank in 4 hours and a second pipe fills 1/5 tank in 3 hours. Combined time to fill whole tank is about:
A tank is 1/4 full. A pipe that fills an empty tank in 12 hours is opened. How many hours to fill completely?
Two inlet pipes and one outlet pipe operate together. Inlet rates are 1/10 and 1/15, outlet is 1/20. Net fill time is:
Pipe A fills a tank in 18 hours, pipe B in 12 hours, and pipe C empties in 36 hours. Net fill time is:
A pipe fills 2/3 of a tank in 8 hours. How many hours for the full tank?
Pipe A works for 3 hours and fills 1/5 tank. Pipe B then works alone and fills the remaining tank in 9 hours. B’s rate is about:
questions.explanation: A filled 1/5 in 3h, remaining 4/5 in 9h, so B rate ≈ 4/45 = 1/11.25. Closest option is 1/15.

questions.question: A pipe fills a tank in 24 hours. Another pipe runs for 6 hours then closes. The remaining tank is filled in 18 more hours. The second pipe alone takes about:
questions.explanation: The second pipe contributed an extra 1/8 of the tank in 6 hours, so rate = 1/48.

questions.question: A cistern is 1/3 full. A pipe fills an empty tank in 15 hours. How long to fill the remaining part?
A pipe fills 1/4 tank in 3 hours and another fills 1/6 tank in 2 hours. Together they fill the whole tank in about:
questions.explanation: Rates are 1/12 and 1/12, combined 1/6, so time = 6 hours.

questions.question: Three pipes fill a tank in 10, 15, and 20 hours. Working together, they fill the tank in about:
Pipe A fills in 12 hours and pipe B in 18 hours. A runs for 3 hours, then B joins. Total time to fill is about:
questions.explanation: A fills 1/4 in 3h. Remaining 3/4 at combined rate 5/36 gives 5.4h more; total 8.4h.

questions.question: A filling pipe takes t hours and an emptying pipe takes 2t hours. Together they fill the tank in:
Pipe A fills in 10 hours and B in 15 hours. They work together for 3 hours, then B stops. How many more hours does A need?
questions.explanation: Work done in 3h = 3/10 + 3/15 = 1/2. Remaining 1/2 by A at 1/10 takes 5 hours.

questions.question: An inlet fills in 12 hours and a leak empties in 20 hours. With both open, the tank fills in:
A tank is 2/5 full. A pipe fills an empty tank in 9 hours. How long to fill the remaining part?
Pipe A fills 2/5 tank in 4 hours and pipe B fills 1/3 tank in 4 hours. Together, the tank fills in about:
A fills in 8 hours and B in 24 hours. They work together for 4 hours, then B stops. How many more hours for A to finish?
questions.explanation: Combined 4h fill 2/3. Remaining 1/3 by A at rate 1/8 takes 8/3 ≈ 2.67 hours, closest answer 3.

questions.question: Three pipes fill a tank in 4, 6, and 12 hours. Their combined time is:
A pipe fills in 16 hours and another empties in 32 hours. With both open, the tank fills in:
